main_components.name: ECCCH
main_components.description: The entire Cultural Heritage Cloud, or ECCCH for short.

main_components.name: Data
main_components.description: This includes the digital heritage assets (digital representations of cultural heritage, such as images, audios and 3D models) that serve as the foundational building blocks for heritage representations within the cloud, as well as the metadata and semantic knowledge accessible through the Cultural Heritage Cloud.

main_components.name: Semantic Resources
main_components.description: This encompasses the conceptual and semantic resources that support the ECCCH ecosystem, including the ECHOES Data Model (Heritage Digital Twin Ontology, HDTO), controlled vocabularies, taxonomies, and related metadata schemas used to describe, interlink, and interpret cultural heritage assets.

main_components.name: Applications and Tools
main_components.description: The applications and other software tools provided by the Cultural Heritage Cloud to support tasks such as data management, visualization, analysis, and user interaction across the ECCCH ecosystem.

main_components.name: Infrastructure and Digital Services
main_components.description: The infrastructure that will provide access to CH data, metadata and digital services in a secure, federated environment. Sample services include user access management, data storage, data sharing, computing resources, search capabilities, workflow management, and Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) services.

main_components.name: Community Services
main_components.description: Community-facing services such as workshops, seminars, and other training, engagement and networking programs to support ECCCH stakeholders.

main_components.name: Workflows
main_components.description: Workflows are structured, potentially automatable sequences of tasks that support the management, analysis, enrichment, preservation, dissemination, and reuse of cultural heritage, enabling their efficient integration and interoperability across the cloud ecosystem.

components_by_evaluable_units.name: ECCCH
components_by_evaluable_units.description: The entire Cultural Heritage Cloud, or ECCCH for short.

components_by_evaluable_units.name: Data
components_by_evaluable_units.description: This includes the digital heritage assets (digital representations of cultural heritage, such as images, audios and 3D models) that serve as the foundational building blocks for heritage representations within the cloud, as well as the metadata and semantic knowledge accessible through the Cultural Heritage Cloud.

components_by_evaluable_units.name: CH Assets
components_by_evaluable_units.description: These are the raw digital materials ("computable data"), such as 3D geometry, text, or audio, that serve as the foundational building blocks for heritage representations within the cloud [D5.3].

components_by_evaluable_units.name: Semantic Metadata and Knowledge
components_by_evaluable_units.description: This encompasses the standardized descriptive information and contextual relationships mapped to the Heritage Digital Twin Ontology (HDTO) to provide machine-readable meaning to assets [D3.1].

components_by_evaluable_units.name: Heritage Digital Twins
components_by_evaluable_units.description: Heritage Digital Twins (HDT) are defined as the integrated sum of both computable data and semantic knowledge that capture the full space-time-culture identity of a heritage asset [D5.3].

components_by_evaluable_units.name: HDT of the Neptune Fountain in Bologna
components_by_evaluable_units.description: A hypothetical HDT of the Neptune Fountain in Bologna.

components_by_evaluable_units.name: Semantic Resources
components_by_evaluable_units.description: This encompasses the conceptual and semantic resources that support the ECCCH ecosystem, including the ECHOES Data Model (Heritage Digital Twin Ontology, HDTO), controlled vocabularies, taxonomies, and related metadata schemas used to describe, interlink, and interpret cultural heritage assets.

components_by_evaluable_units.name: Ontologies
components_by_evaluable_units.description: Ontologies that define the entities and relationships used to describe cultural heritage assets and their properties.

components_by_evaluable_units.name: Heritage Digital Twin Ontology
components_by_evaluable_units.description: The Heritage Digital Twin Ontology (HDTO) is the data model, based on CIDOC CRM, that will be used in the ECCCH Knowledge Graph to unify descriptions and facilitate query and navigation.

components_by_evaluable_units.name: Controlled Vocabularies
components_by_evaluable_units.description: Structured collections of standardized terms that ensure consistent description and classification of cultural heritage data.

components_by_evaluable_units.name: Semantic Mappings
components_by_evaluable_units.description: Mappings that define correspondences between ontologies, enabling their interoperability.

components_by_evaluable_units.name: Applications and Tools
components_by_evaluable_units.description: The applications and other software tools provided by the Cultural Heritage Cloud to support tasks such as data management, visualization, analysis, and user interaction across the ECCCH ecosystem.

components_by_evaluable_units.name: OCRA
components_by_evaluable_units.description: OCRA (Online Conservation and Restoration Annotator) is a cloud-integrated tool for annotating and documenting the conservation and restoration of cultural heritage assets.

components_by_evaluable_units.name: VTL
components_by_evaluable_units.description: The VTL (Virtual Transcription Laboratory) is a cloud-based tool that supports the collaborative transcription, annotation, and enrichment of digitized cultural heritage texts.

components_by_evaluable_units.name: CIT
components_by_evaluable_units.description: The CIT (Collection Integration Tool) is a cloud-based application that enables the aggregation, harmonization, and enrichment of cultural heritage collections within the ECHOES ecosystem.

components_by_evaluable_units.name: Other Vertical Applications
components_by_evaluable_units.description: Vertical applications from sister projects, cascading grants and other initiatives.

components_by_evaluable_units.name: Infrastructure and Digital Services
components_by_evaluable_units.description: The infrastructure that will provide access to CH data, metadata and digital services in a secure, federated environment. Sample services include user access management, data storage, data sharing, computing resources, search capabilities, workflow management, and Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) services.

components_by_evaluable_units.name: System services
components_by_evaluable_units.description: This includes the SEP gateway, Authentication and Authorization, and other system-level services.

components_by_evaluable_units.name: Storage services
components_by_evaluable_units.description: Includes RDF triple stores, user space and other storage-related services.

components_by_evaluable_units.name: Cloud services
components_by_evaluable_units.description: Services for monitoring, resource management, node management, etc.

components_by_evaluable_units.name: Community Services
components_by_evaluable_units.description: Community-facing services such as workshops, seminars, and other training, engagement and networking programs to support ECCCH stakeholders.

components_by_evaluable_units.name: Training and Capacity-Building Services
components_by_evaluable_units.description: Programs, workshops, and learning resources designed to strengthen the digital, technical, and heritage management skills of ECCCH stakeholders.

components_by_evaluable_units.name: Engagement and Networking Services
components_by_evaluable_units.description: Activities and platforms that promote community participation, knowledge exchange, and collaboration among cultural heritage professionals, researchers, and institutions.

components_by_evaluable_units.name: "Introduction to the cloud" course
components_by_evaluable_units.description: A course offered by ECHOES that introduces CH professionals, researchers, and developers to the purpose, core concepts, and practical applications of the ECCCH.

components_by_evaluable_units.name: Workflows
components_by_evaluable_units.description: Workflows are structured, potentially automatable sequences of tasks that support the management, analysis, enrichment, preservation, dissemination, and reuse of cultural heritage, enabling their efficient integration and interoperability across the cloud ecosystem.

components_by_evaluable_units.name: Text-based workflows
components_by_evaluable_units.description: Workflows documented as textual instructions that are executed manually, providing basic knowledge sharing but limited reproducibility and automation [D3.2].

components_by_evaluable_units.name: Script-based workflows
components_by_evaluable_units.description: Script based workflows that encode execution logic and can be reused across compatible environments, improving portability, versioning, and partial reproducibility [D3.2].

components_by_evaluable_units.name: Graph-based Workflows
components_by_evaluable_units.description: Fully executable and orchestrated workflows managed by the cloud, enabling end to end automation, deep provenance tracking, and federated execution across multiple nodes [D3.2].

components_by_evaluable_units.name: Massive Transcription Workflow
components_by_evaluable_units.description: Hypothetical workflow based on a workflow engine that massively ingests images in one country and performs OCR and processing in another one [D3.1].

categories.name: Cloud
categories.description: Top-level category covering all parts of the ECCCH

categories.name: Data
categories.description: Information and datasets managed in the cloud

categories.name: Knowledge graph data
categories.description: Structured semantic data linking heritage entities

categories.name: Ontology
categories.description: Formal conceptual models of a domain

categories.name: Vocabulary
categories.description: Controlled lists of terms and definitions

categories.name: Semantic mapping
categories.description: Correspondences between different schemas or vocabularies

categories.name: Infrastructure and services
categories.description: Underlying hardware, system resources and digital services

categories.name: Community service
categories.description: Support and engagement resources for users

categories.name: User support service
categories.description: Help and assistance for platform users

categories.name: Workshop service
categories.description: Interactive events for collaboration and learning

categories.name: Workflow
categories.description: Defined sequences of processes and tasks

categories.name: Text-based workflows
categories.description: Workflows documented as textual instructions that are executed manually, providing basic knowledge sharing but limited reproducibility and automation [D3.2].

categories.name: Script-based workflows
categories.description: Script based workflows that encode execution logic and can be reused across compatible environments, improving portability, versioning, and partial reproducibility [D3.2].

categories.name: Graph-based Workflows
categories.description: Fully executable and orchestrated workflows managed by the cloud, enabling end to end automation, deep provenance tracking, and federated execution across multiple nodes [D3.2].

data_dim.name: Data usage
data_dim.description: This dimension assesses how datasets, metadata, and paradata are accessed, reused, and integrated within the ECCCH ecosystem, providing insight into their relevance, adoption, and impact.

data_dim.name: Access frequency
data_dim.description: Measures how often a dataset or resource is accessed over a given period of time, including views, queries, or downloads, indicating its level of visibility and demand.
data_dim.example: (e.g. number of views/downloads over a period; unique vs. returning accesses; peak access times...)

data_dim.name: User demographics
data_dim.description: Characterizes the types of users accessing the data (e.g., researchers, cultural heritage professionals, educators, general public), enabling analysis of reach across different stakeholder groups.
data_dim.example: (e.g. geographic distribution of users accessing data; profile of such users...)

data_dim.name: User engagement
data_dim.description: Evaluates the depth and quality of interaction with the data, including session duration, repeated access, queries performed, or reuse in workflows and applications.
data_dim.example: (e.g. average session duration per HDT, number of HDTs accessed per session, interactions such as annotations, comments, or user-contributed metadata)

data_dim.name: Integration rate
data_dim.description: Measures the extent to which datasets are integrated into applications, workflows, knowledge graphs, or other services within the ECCCH, indicating their level of reuse and interoperability in practice.
data_dim.example: (e.g. number of times a HDT is integrated into applications, projects, or digital exhibitions; citations of a HDT in publications or presentations)

metadata_dim.name: Metadata Quality
metadata_dim.description: Degree to which metadata fulfills its role of identifying and describing digital resources in a manner that is understandable by both machines and people.
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Intrinsic Quality
metadata_dim.description: Concerns the inherent properties of metadata that can be evaluated independently of the user's needs or external context; measures whether the metadata is factually correct, coherent, and trustworthy based only on the information within the dataset.
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Accuracy
metadata_dim.description: Measures how well metadata represent real-world entities.
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Factual Accuracy (Correctness)
metadata_dim.description: Verifies whether metadata values are free from errors (correct names, dates, identifiers, spatial coordinates, etc.); ensures RDF triples represent facts and the metadata correctly describes the actual resource.
metadata_dim.example: In Wikidata, the entity Q42 (Douglas Adams) has shown incorrect values for his place of death or birth due to user edits
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Semantic Accuracy
metadata_dim.description: Checks whether metadata correctly conveys the meaning and interpretation of the associated resource; assesses whether the meaning conveyed is accurate and appropriate (interpretation, categorization, or alignment between fields and resources).
metadata_dim.example: A book about women in science during the 19th century described with the subject ”biography”, when in fact the subject is ”thematic historical study”
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Believability (Uncertainty)
metadata_dim.description: Confirms whether uncertainty is explicitly represented and modeled transparently.
metadata_dim.example: Wikidata indicates doubts by storing properties with ”low-confidence” qualifiers (e.g., conflicting birth years)
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Consistency
metadata_dim.description: Relates to the coherence of metadata and the absence of contradictions among statements; data should adhere to logical rules defined by the underlying data model (e.g., domain and range constraints). Evaluates as well, whether free-text fields of the same resource (e.g., title, description, subject) describe the same object in a semantically similar way, preventing confusing or contradictory descriptions.
metadata_dim.example: In CIDOCCRM (version 7.1.3), the property P11 had participant / participated in has domain E5 Event and range E39 Actor. A correct example of consistency would be Napoleon Bonaparte (E39 Actor) participated in the French Revolution (E5 Event). By contrast, an inconsistent statement would be Mona Lisa (a physical object violating the domain) participated in Paris (a place, violating the range). An item having the title ”Memorial Statue of Queen Victoria”, providing a description such as ”Bronze sculpture of unknown woman”
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Trustworthiness and Provenance
metadata_dim.description: Evaluates the reliability and credibility of the metadata source.
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Provenance and Credibility
metadata_dim.description: Metadata contains information about the origin of the data, including who created it, their expertise, sources used, and any transformation applied.
metadata_dim.example: A reference URL to an unreliable blog or a missing webpage
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Traceability (Verifiability)
metadata_dim.description: Validates that metadata statements can be connected to authoritative sources (e.g., catalog entries, archival documents, publications, or external datasets).
metadata_dim.example: A DBpedia triple asserting that an author won an award without providing citations or a reference to the Wikipedia source
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Contextual Quality
metadata_dim.description: Verifies whether metadata is sufficient, timely, and fitting for its domain (beyond being correct).
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Completeness
metadata_dim.description: Ensures that metadata contains all the information required.
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Structural Completeness
metadata_dim.description: Assesses whether individual records contain values for all required metadata fields defined by the schema; can be measured as ratio of filled fields to expected fields.
metadata_dim.example: A book that contains title, author, and publication date as fields, but lacks the ISBN identifier
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Schema Completeness
metadata_dim.description: Validates whether the metadata model itself is appropriately used to describe a given type of resource; assesses whether relevant classes and properties defined in the schema are employed across the dataset.
metadata_dim.example: People described without birth place despite schema support.
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Population Completeness
metadata_dim.description: Assesses the extent to which real-world objects are represented in the dataset.
metadata_dim.example: If a museum has 500 artifacts, but only 350 are published, population completeness is 70%.
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Richness (Information Content)
metadata_dim.description: Evaluates the amount and diversity of descriptive information available for a resource; adds a gradual, qualitative layer beyond the minimum ensured by structural completeness.
metadata_dim.example: Consider two Wikidata entries for historical monuments: one with detailed architectural style, heritage designation, materials, and images; another with only the title and the location. Although both entities are considered complete at a basic level, the second one provides only a minimal identification and lacks descriptive richness
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Multilingual Labeling Coverage
metadata_dim.description: Validates the presence, correctness, and consistency of multilingual metadata; includes appropriate language tags and consistency across multilingual descriptions.
metadata_dim.example: Multilingual environments require language-tagged labels for search and discovery
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Timeliness
metadata_dim.description: Ensures that metadata remains up-to-date and relevant to the present; modification dates should be documented to avoid outdated contradictory knowledge.
metadata_dim.example:
An item representing a politician might still contain an outdated position (e.g., Mayor from 2010 to 2014)
Records associated with a museum might have been moved or loaned to another institution without being updated.
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Context and Relevance
metadata_dim.description: Measures whether the metadata is useful for the intended audience or use case; assesses whether the information meets the user's needs.
metadata_dim.example: Metadata designed for cultural heritage programs may prioritize visual material and narrative descriptions, while metadata intended for scholarly research should include references to primary sources and detailed provenance.
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Representational Quality
metadata_dim.description: Evaluates how well the data is expressed, structured, and presented; focuses on readability, interpretability, structural representation, and the quality of language.
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Interpretability and Understandability
metadata_dim.description: Evaluates how easily interpretable by human users and machine agents metadata is; identifies poor wording, lack of context, and ambiguous phrasing.
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: General Understandability
metadata_dim.description: Assesses whether data is presented clearly and can be easily interpreted by its intended audience (user or machine); influenced by schema usage and clarity of labels, identifiers, and short descriptive fields.
metadata_dim.example:
A record using abbreviated or highly
technical codes (e.g., loc crt: FR-75) without
explanation may be difficult to interpret, even if it is technically correct
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Cognitive Accessibility (Readability)
metadata_dim.description: Checks whether textual metadata is easy to read; focuses on the cognitive effort required to read it (assuming it is already interpretable).
metadata_dim.example: Long, technical, or machine-translated abstracts with reduced readability for general audiences
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Structure
metadata_dim.description: Concerned with the syntactic and structural correctness of metadata; validates representation types including URIs and RDF constructs.
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Foundational and Format Consistency
metadata_dim.description: Checks whether metadata respects structural and syntactic rules of the underlying data model (datatype compliance, correct use of object vs. datatype properties, valid/resolvable URIs, valid language-tagged strings).
metadata_dim.example: Textual fields, for instance, are evaluated only with respect to their datatype and encoding (valid string literals, language tags)
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Conciseness (Absence of Redundancy)
metadata_dim.description: Evaluates how efficiently metadata describes resources; checks for unnecessary redundancy such as duplicate entities under multiple identifiers or repeated equivalent values.
metadata_dim.example:
Identical entities under multiple identifiers or repetition of properties with equivalent
values
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Accessibility Quality
metadata_dim.description: Assesses how easily the data can be found, accessed, and reused.
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Accessibility and Connectivity
metadata_dim.description: Ensures metadata can be accessed reliably in Knowledge Graphs and Linked Data; checks dereferenceable URIs, reachable resources, downloadable metadata files in standard formats, and stable/responsive endpoints.
metadata_dim.example: Broken URIs, inaccessible endpoints, or missing download links.
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Governance and Reusability
metadata_dim.description: Ensures metadata can be reused, shared, and maintained long-term.
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Interlinking (Connectivity)
metadata_dim.description: Verifies both internal and external metadata links, including links to authority files, equivalences, and internal links between related resources.
metadata_dim.example: Artist not linked to authority file (e.g., VIAF).
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Licensing (Rights)
metadata_dim.description: Checks that metadata includes accurate information about rights, permissions, and licensing, which determines whether metadata can be reused and under what conditions.
metadata_dim.example: Image without license information.
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Interoperability (Shareability)
metadata_dim.description: Evaluates the ease with which metadata can be shared across systems/platforms/institutions; assesses use of common standards, adherence to controlled vocabularies, and compatibility with Linked Data technologies.
metadata_dim.example: Two knowledge graphs using different vocabularies for the same concept: one referring to the creator as schema:creator, and the other one as dc:creator
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Conformance to Standards
metadata_dim.description: Assesses whether metadata follows community standards, schemas, and best practices, supporting reusability and automated validation.
metadata_dim.example: Records not following EDM.
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

metadata_dim.name: Versionability
metadata_dim.description: Capacity to maintain multiple versions of the same record, track changes over time, and allow rollback in case of errors.
metadata_dim.example: No record of who changed a description and when.
metadata_dim.contributors: Alexandra Sicobean (UPC), Imanol Muñoz-Pandiella (UPC), Carlos Andujar (UPC)

ontology_dim.name: Comprehensiveness
ontology_dim.description: The extent to which the ontology can represent cultural heritage information in a sufficiently broad and appropriate way. This includes coverage of relevant asset types, entities, relationships, and properties across different cultural heritage domains and use cases.

ontology_dim.name: Usability and Understandability
ontology_dim.description: The extent to which the ontology can be understood, adopted, and applied effectively by stakeholders. This includes conceptual clarity, accessibility, quality of documentation, and practical usefulness.

ontology_dim.name: Interoperability and Compatibility
ontology_dim.description: The extent to which the ontology can be integrated and used with other models, standards, and systems. This includes alignment with established data models, controlled vocabularies, and relevant standards, as well as technical compatibility through supported formats and interfaces.

ontology_dim.name: Usage and Impact
ontology_dim.description: The extent to which the ontology is adopted, reused, and influential in cultural heritage research and practice, including its contribution to collaboration within the ECCCH ecosystem.

ontology_dim.name: Sustainability
ontology_dim.description: The extent to which the ontology can remain viable over time, including ease of maintenance, adaptability to new domains or standards, scalability, and the existence of appropriate governance and versioning practices.

software_dim.name: Software dimensions
software_dim.description: Software

software_dim.name: Compatibility
software_dim.description: Degree to which a product, system or component can exchange information with other products, systems or components, and/or perform its required functions while sharing the same common environment and resources.

software_dim.name: Co-existence
software_dim.description: Degree to which a product can perform its required functions efficiently while sharing a common environment and resources with other products, without detrimental impact on any other product.

software_dim.name: Interoperability
software_dim.description: Degree to which a system, product or component can exchange information with other products and mutually use the information that has been exchanged.

software_dim.name: FAIRness
software_dim.description: Degree to which research software adheres to the FAIR principles: Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable. These principles, adapted for research software, aim to enhance the discoverability, accessibility, interoperability, and reusability of software, thereby maximizing its value and impact in scientific research.
software_dim.source_name: Introducing the FAIR Principles for research software

software_dim.name: Flexibility
software_dim.description: Degree to which a product can be adapted to changes in its requirements, contexts of use or system environment.

software_dim.name: Adaptability
software_dim.description: Degree to which a product or system can effectively and efficiently be adapted for or transferred to different hardware, software or other operational or usage environments.

software_dim.name: Scalability
software_dim.description: Degree to which a product can handle growing or shrinking workloads or to adapt its capacity to handle variability.

software_dim.name: Installability
software_dim.description: Degree of effectiveness and efficiency with which a product or system can be successfully installed and/or uninstalled in a specified environment.

software_dim.name: Replaceability
software_dim.description: Degree to which a product can replace another specified software product for the same purpose in the same environment.

software_dim.name: Functional suitability
software_dim.description: This characteristic represents the degree to which a product or system provides functions that meet stated and implied needs when used under specified conditions.

software_dim.name: Functional completeness
software_dim.description: Degree to which the set of functions covers all the specified tasks and intended users’ objectives.

software_dim.name: Functional correctness
software_dim.description: Degree to which a product or system provides accurate results when used by intended users.

software_dim.name: Functional appropriateness
software_dim.description: Degree to which the functions facilitate the accomplishment of specified tasks and objectives.

software_dim.name: Interaction Capability
software_dim.description: Degree to which a product or system can be interacted with by specified users to exchange information via the user interface to complete specific tasks in a variety of contexts of use.

software_dim.name: Appropriateness recognizability
software_dim.description: Degree to which users can recognize whether a product or system is appropriate for their needs.

software_dim.name: Learnability
software_dim.description: Degree to which the functions of a product or system can be learnt to be used by specified users within a specified amount of time.

software_dim.name: Operability
software_dim.description: Degree to which a product or system has attributes that make it easy to operate and control.

software_dim.name: User error protection
software_dim.description: Degree to which a system prevents users against operation errors.

software_dim.name: User engagement
software_dim.description: Degree to which a user interface presents functions and information in an inviting and motivating manner encouraging continued interaction.

software_dim.name: Inclusivity
software_dim.description: Degree to which a product or system can be used by people of various backgrounds (such as people of various ages, abilities, cultures, ethnicities, languages, genders, economic situations, etc.).

software_dim.name: User assistance
software_dim.description: Degree to which a product can be used by people with the widest range of characteristics and capabilities to achieve specified goals in a specified context of use.

software_dim.name: Self-descriptiveness
software_dim.description: Degree to which a product presents appropriate information, where needed by the user, to make its capabilities and use immediately obvious to the user without excessive interactions with a product or other resources (such as user documentation, help desks or other users).

software_dim.name: Maintainability
software_dim.description: his characteristic represents the degree of effectiveness and efficiency with which a product or system can be modified to improve it, correct it or adapt it to changes in environment, and in requirements.

software_dim.name: Modularity
software_dim.description: Degree to which a system or computer program is composed of discrete components such that a change to one component has minimal impact on other components.

software_dim.name: Reusability
software_dim.description: Degree to which a product can be used as an asset in more than one system, or in building other assets.

software_dim.name: Analysability
software_dim.description: Degree of effectiveness and efficiency with which it is possible to assess the impact on a product or system of an intended change to one or more of its parts, to diagnose a product for deficiencies or causes of failures, or to identify parts to be modified.

software_dim.name: Modifiability
software_dim.description: Degree to which a product or system can be effectively and efficiently modified without introducing defects or degrading existing product quality.

software_dim.name: Testability
software_dim.description: Degree of effectiveness and efficiency with which test criteria can be established for a system, product or component and tests can be performed to determine whether those criteria have been met.

software_dim.name: Open Source Software
software_dim.description: Degree to which software source code is openly available for inspection, use, modification, and redistribution under an open licence. Open source software is software with source code that anyone can inspect, modify, and enhance. Research software can be published with or without open access to the source code. Open access to source code aligns better with academic research purposes than closed source software; open source software aligns with the FAIR4RS principles. It allows other researchers to directly verify the methods used to produce the results published in papers. It also makes reproducibility much easier. In addition to these research-driven reasons, publishing research software as open source software can help with long term maintenance in a cost-effective way, since interested developers can easily contribute new functionality or fix bugs. Moreover, by integrating with the greater open source ecosystem, researchers can leverage tools and support communities already available. As such, for most academic communities with limited resources, it is also a good choice from a software engineering perspective

software_dim.name: Performance Efficiency
software_dim.description: This characteristic represents the degree to which a product performs its functions within specified time and throughput parameters and is efficient in the use of resources (such as CPU, memory, storage, network devices, energy, materials…) under specified conditions.

software_dim.name: Time behaviour
software_dim.description: Degree to which the response time and throughput rates of a product or system, when performing its functions, meet requirements.

software_dim.name: Resource utilization
software_dim.description: Degree to which the amounts and types of resources used by a product or system, when performing its functions, meet requirements.

software_dim.name: Capacity
software_dim.description: Degree to which the maximum limits of a product or system parameter meet requirements.

software_dim.name: Reliability
software_dim.description: Degree to which a system, product or component performs specified functions under specified conditions for a specified period of time.

software_dim.name: Faultlessness
software_dim.description: Degree to which a system, product or component performs specified functions without fault under normal operation.

software_dim.name: Availability
software_dim.description: Degree to which a system, product or component is operational and accessible when required for use.

software_dim.name: Fault tolerance
software_dim.description: Degree to which a system, product or component operates as intended despite the presence of hardware or software faults.

software_dim.name: Recoverability
software_dim.description: Degree to which, in the event of an interruption or a failure, a product or system can recover the data directly affected and re-establish the desired state of the system.

software_dim.name: Safety
software_dim.description: This characteristic represents the degree to which a product under defined conditions avoids a state in which human life, health, property, or the environment is endangered.

software_dim.name: Operational constraint
software_dim.description: Degree to which a product or system constrains its operation to within safe parameters or states when encountering operational hazard.

software_dim.name: Risk identification
software_dim.description: Degree to which a product can identify a course of events or operations that can expose life, property or environment to unacceptable risk.

software_dim.name: Fail safe
software_dim.description: Degree to which a product can automatically place itself in a safe operating mode, or to revert to a safe condition in the event of a failure.

software_dim.name: Hazard warning
software_dim.description: Degree to which a product or system provides warnings of unacceptable risks to operations or internal controls so that they can react in sufficient time to sustain safe operations.

software_dim.name: Safe integration
software_dim.description: Degree to which a product can maintain safety during and after integration with one or more components.

software_dim.name: Security
software_dim.description: Degree to which a product or system defends against attack patterns by malicious actors and protects information and data so that persons or other products or systems have the degree of data access appropriate to their types and levels of authorization.

software_dim.name: Confidentiality
software_dim.description: Degree to which a product or system ensures that data are accessible only to those authorized to have access.

software_dim.name: Integrity
software_dim.description: Degree to which a system, product or component ensures that the state of its system and data are protected from unauthorized modification or deletion either by malicious action or computer error.

software_dim.name: Non-repudiation
software_dim.description: Degree to which actions or events can be proven to have taken place so that the events or actions cannot be repudiated later.

software_dim.name: Accountability
software_dim.description: Degree to which the actions of an entity can be traced uniquely to the entity.

software_dim.name: Authenticity
software_dim.description: Degree to which the identity of a subject or resource can be proved to be the one claimed.

software_dim.name: Resistance
software_dim.description: Degree to which the product or system sustains operations while under attack from a malicious actor.

software_dim.name: Sustainability
software_dim.description: Degree to which software can continue to exist, be maintained, and remain useful over time across changing platforms, dependencies, and user needs.

software_dim.name: Usability
software_dim.description: This dimension refers to how easy, efficient, and satisfactory the ECCCH applications and tools are for their intended users.

software_dim.name: Usefulness
software_dim.description: Measures the extent to which the application effectively addresses the specific needs and goals of CH professionals.

software_dim.name: Efficiency of Use
software_dim.description: Measures how quickly and effectively experienced users can accomplish tasks with the tools.

software_dim.name: Ease of Learning
software_dim.description: Indicates how quickly new users can understand and effectively operate the application.

software_dim.name: User Interface Quality
software_dim.description: Assesses the intuitiveness, consistency, and visual clarity of the application's design and layout.

software_dim.name: Documentation quality
software_dim.description: Evaluates the clarity, comprehensiveness, and accessibility of user guides, tutorials, FAQs, and other support materials about the application.

software_dim.name: Compliance
software_dim.description: This dimension evaluates the adherence of the application to established standards, regulations, and project-specific guidelines. Subdimensions are very preliminar.

software_dim.name: Access Management for IPR
software_dim.description: Evaluates the effectiveness of the application in managing and restricting access based on intellectual propert

software_dim.name: Licensing Clarity
software_dim.description: Measures the application's ability to enforce explicit licensing terms for all data.

software_dim.name: Data quality and Provenance
software_dim.description: Measures the application's ability to ensure data quality, consistency, and clear traceability of its origin and modifications.

software_dim.name: Data Governance and IPR enforcement
software_dim.description: This dimension focuses on how the application facilitates or enforces the data governance model and the preservation of intellectual property rights.

software_dim.name: Standard Adherence
software_dim.description: Assesses conformity with relevant technical standards, data formats, and open models.

software_dim.name: Regulatory Alignment
software_dim.description: Measures compliance with national, European, and international legal and ethical frameworks.

software_dim.name: Guideline Compliance
software_dim.description: Ensures adherence to specific integration, development, and communication guidelines provided by the ECHOES consortium.

software_dim.name: Learning value for developers
software_dim.description: This dimension considers how well the application supports and educates developers willing to contribute with their own applications.

software_dim.name: Developer Documentation Quality
software_dim.description: Assesses the clarity, comprehensiveness, and utility of technical documentation tailored for developers, including examples and guidelines.

software_dim.name: Training Resources Availability
software_dim.description: Evaluates the provision of educational materials, tutorials, and capacity-building activities specifically for developers to enhance their skills.

software_dim.name: Community Support and Collaboration
software_dim.description: Measures the effectiveness of mechanisms that facilitate knowledge exchange, problem-solving, and collaborative development among the developer community regarding a specific application.

software_dim.name: User Adoption
software_dim.description: This dimension measures the extent to which CH stakeholders embrace and effectively use a specific application.

software_dim.name: Awareness and Understanding
software_dim.description: Reflects how well target communities are informed about and comprehend the advantages of using a specific ECCCH application.

software_dim.name: Engagement Rate
software_dim.description: Indicates the frequency and depth of active use of the application by users (active users, session duration...).

software_dim.name: Acceptance Rate
software_dim.description: Measures the overall willingness and readiness of the community to integrate and use a specific application in their workflows.

software_dim.name: Training Effectiveness
software_dim.description: Evaluates the success of capacity-building activities and the ECHOES curriculum in equipping users with the necessary skills for using the application.

service_dim.name: Availability
service_dim.description: This dimension assesses the extent to which the digital services are continuously operational and accessible, minimizing downtime and ensuring users can reliably access CH resources at any time.

service_dim.name: Service Uptime
service_dim.description: Measures the proportion of time that the services remain fully operational and accessible to users.

service_dim.name: Redundancy and Failover Mechanisms
service_dim.description: Assesses the presence and effectiveness of backup systems, load balancing, and failover strategies that ensure service continuity in case of component failure.

service_dim.name: Incident Response Time
service_dim.description: Evaluates how quickly the infrastructure team can detect, respond to, and resolve outages or service interruptions.

service_dim.name: Monitoring and Alerting
service_dim.description: Examines the robustness of monitoring systems and real-time alert mechanisms that track service health and performance.

service_dim.name: Reliability
service_dim.description: This dimension evaluates the infrastructure’s ability to deliver consistent and dependable service performance under expected and peak workloads, avoiding system failures and data loss.

service_dim.name: Fault Tolerance
service_dim.description: Assesses the infrastructure’s capacity to continue operating correctly even when components fail or experience errors.

service_dim.name: Data Integrity
service_dim.description: Evaluates mechanisms ensuring that data remains accurate, complete, and consistent during storage, processing, and transmission.

service_dim.name: Backup and Recovery Procedures
service_dim.description: Measures the effectiveness and frequency of data backups and the ability to restore systems quickly after a failure or data loss.

service_dim.name: Service Consistency
service_dim.description: Examines the ability of the infrastructure to provide predictable and stable service performance under varying workloads and operational conditions.

service_dim.name: Performance
service_dim.description: This dimension measures the responsiveness, speed, and efficiency of the infrastructure and its services in processing, storing, and retrieving CH data and metadata.

service_dim.name: Response Time
service_dim.description: Measures how quickly the infrastructure and its services respond to user actions or data requests.

service_dim.name: Throughput
service_dim.description: Evaluates the volume of operations, transactions, or data processed by the infrastructure within a given time period.

service_dim.name: Resource Utilization Efficiency
service_dim.description: Assesses how effectively the system uses available computing resources (CPU, memory, storage, and network) to deliver optimal performance.

service_dim.name: Scalability Under Load
service_dim.description: Examines the infrastructure’s ability to maintain or improve performance as the number of users, data volume, or computational demand increases.

service_dim.name: Security
service_dim.description: This dimension evaluates the mechanisms protecting data, metadata, and services from unauthorized access, alteration, or loss, ensuring confidentiality, integrity, and compliance with relevant security standards.

service_dim.name: Access Control
service_dim.description: Assesses the effectiveness of authentication and authorization mechanisms that manage and restrict user access to systems and data.

service_dim.name: Data Protection
service_dim.description: Evaluates the safeguards ensuring confidentiality, integrity, and availability of CH data during storage, transfer, and processing.

service_dim.name: Vulnerability Management
service_dim.description: Measures the processes for identifying, assessing, and mitigating security risks, vulnerabilities, and potential threats within the infrastructure.

service_dim.name: Compliance and Auditing
service_dim.description: Examines adherence to relevant security standards and regulations, and the implementation of regular audits to verify compliance and detect anomalies.

software_quality_tools.name: ISO/IEC 25010 software product quality model
software_quality_tools.dimensions_covered: Functional suitability, performance efficiency, compatibility, usability, reliability, security, maintainability, portability.
software_quality_tools.description: Defines a standardised model of software product quality characteristics and sub-characteristics, providing a common vocabulary and reference framework for specifying, evaluating and comparing software quality.

other.License: ISO standards are proprietary documents. Full access requires purchase, and the text is protected by copyright; redistribution or republication of the content is not permitted.

software_quality_tools.name: ISO/IEC 25023 measurement of system and software product quality
software_quality_tools.dimensions_covered: Quantitative measures for maintainability, reliability, performance efficiency, functional suitability, usability and other ISO 25010 characteristics
software_quality_tools.description: Provides a standard catalog of quantitative measures for assessing system and software product quality characteristics defined in ISO/IEC 25010.

other.License: ISO standards are proprietary documents. Full access requires purchase, and the text is protected by copyright; redistribution or republication of the content is not permitted.

software_quality_tools.name: ISO/IEC 5055 Automated Source Code Quality Measures
software_quality_tools.dimensions_covered: Maintainability, reliability, performance efficiency, security (structural quality of source code)
software_quality_tools.description: Defines automated measures for assessing the structural quality of source code, focusing on maintainability, reliability, performance efficiency and security weaknesses.

other.Type of tool: International standard; automated source-code quality measurement model
other.License: ISO standards are proprietary documents. Full access requires purchase, and the text is protected by copyright; redistribution or republication of the content is not permitted.

software_quality_tools.name: OMG ASCQM (Automated Source Code Quality Measures) v1.1
software_quality_tools.description: OMG specification for automatically measuring software structural quality through static source-code analysis. It defines a catalog of weaknesses and detection patterns; scores per quality dimension are computed by counting and aggregating occurrences of these weaknesses by quality characteristic. The specification also defines conformance requirements to ensure that measurements are automated, objective, transparent, and repeatable.

other.Type of tool: OMG specification; automated source-code structural quality measurement model (static-analysis-based)
other.License: Copyrighted OMG specification; publicly downloadable, but redistribution, modification, or republication of the text is restricted by license.

software_quality_tools.name: Radon (Python code metrics)
software_quality_tools.dimensions_covered: Maintainability index, cyclomatic complexity, Halstead metrics, source lines of code, comment density
software_quality_tools.description: Computes code complexity, maintainability and size metrics for Python codebases to support early identification of hard-to-maintain modules.

software_quality_tools.name: Visual Studio (IDE) Code Metrics (Maintainability Index and related metrics)
software_quality_tools.dimensions_covered: Maintainability index, cyclomatic complexity, depth of inheritance, class coupling, lines of code
software_quality_tools.description: Provides IDE-integrated metrics for analysing maintainability, complexity and structural properties of object-oriented code, mainly for .NET projects.

software_quality_tools.name: Chidamber Kemerer (CK) metrics suite (e.g. via CKJM or similar tools)
software_quality_tools.dimensions_covered: Object-oriented design quality: complexity, inheritance depth, coupling, cohesion, potential fault-proneness and maintainability
software_quality_tools.description: Offers a widely used suite of object-oriented design metrics to assess complexity, coupling, cohesion and inheritance-related maintainability risks.

software_quality_tools.name: DORA Four Key Metrics (Four Keys)
software_quality_tools.dimensions_covered: Change failure rate, mean time to recovery (MTTR), deployment frequency, lead time for changes
software_quality_tools.description: Defines a set of four key metrics to measure software delivery performance and operational reliability in DevOps and continuous delivery environments.

other.Type of tool: Process/performance metric model and associated data collection tooling

software_quality_tools.name: DORA “Documentation quality” capability
software_quality_tools.dimensions_covered: Quality of internal documentation: clarity, completeness, findability, accuracy, up-to-dateness
software_quality_tools.description: Provides a capability-based assessment framework to evaluate the quality of internal documentation using structured, survey-based indicators.

software_quality_tools.name: OpenSSF Best Practices Badge
software_quality_tools.dimensions_covered: Project-level practices covering documentation, testing, code review, security, CI, issue management and release process
software_quality_tools.description: Provides a checklist-based assessment and public badge to evaluate and communicate adherence to recognised best practices in open-source software projects.

other.Type of tool: Checklist and badging system for FLOSS project practices

software_quality_tools.name: FAIR for Research Software (FAIR4RS) Principles
software_quality_tools.dimensions_covered: Findability, accessibility, interoperability, reusability of research software and associated documentation and metadata
software_quality_tools.description: Defines principles and assessment criteria to improve the findability, accessibility, interoperability and reusability of research software.

software_quality_tools.name: SPDX specification
software_quality_tools.description: SPDX is a standardized format for describing the components of a software system. It provides a common way for development teams to document what is included in their software, typically in the form of a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM).

software_functional_tools.name: ISO/IEC 25040 – Evaluation Process (SQuaRE)
software_functional_tools.dimensions_covered: Process for evaluating all quality characteristics, including functional suitability
software_functional_tools.description: Defines a standard process model for planning, executing and concluding software quality evaluations, ensuring that assessments are systematic, repeatable and auditable.

software_functional_tools.name: Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)
software_functional_tools.dimensions_covered: Coverage of functional requirements; implementation and test status (completeness and correctness)
software_functional_tools.description: Provides a structured artefact to map functional requirements to implementation and test artefacts, supporting monitoring of functional completeness and correctness.

other.Degree of automation: partially automated (via ALM tools) but requiring manual maintenance

software_functional_tools.name: Automated functional test suites + coverage tools (e.g. JUnit/pytest/Jest with JaCoCo, coverage.py, Istanbul)
software_functional_tools.dimensions_covered: Pass/fail status of functional behaviour; test coverage (correctness, partial completeness)
software_functional_tools.description: Provides automated execution and measurement of functional tests to verify correctness of implemented behaviour and quantify test coverage of functional requirements.

other.Degree of automation: highly automated execution within CI pipelines

software_functional_tools.name: OpenAPI-based API contract testing (e.g. Dredd, Schemathesis)
software_functional_tools.dimensions_covered: Conformance of API behaviour to specified contract; robustness and correctness of API-level functions
software_functional_tools.description: Provides automated validation of API behaviour against formally specified OpenAPI contracts to ensure functional correctness and robustness at the interface level.

software_functional_tools.name: Property-based / fuzz testing libraries (e.g. Hypothesis; Schemathesis property-based mode)
software_functional_tools.dimensions_covered: Correctness and robustness of functional behaviour under varied and edge-case inputs
software_functional_tools.description: Provides automated generation and execution of test cases based on properties and invariants to detect functional defects under a wide range of input conditions.

other.Degree of automation: largely automated test generation and execution

software_functional_tools.name: Task-based user test protocol
software_functional_tools.dimensions_covered: Task success, errors, efficiency, alignment of functions with real workflows (appropriateness, plus completeness/correctness checks)
software_functional_tools.description: Provides a scenario-based testing protocol to evaluate whether users can successfully complete representative tasks using available system functions.
software_functional_tools.link: (can be implemented with tools such as open-source screen recording combined with log analysis)

other.Degree of automation: user-based observation; metrics collection can be semi-automated (screen recording, logging)

software_e2e_testing_tools.name: Selenium WebDriver
software_e2e_testing_tools.description: Widely used open-source framework for automating web browsers and performing functional end-to-end tests using the W3C WebDriver standard

other.Interactions: Mouse clicks, typing into inputs, selecting from menus, form submission, navigation across pages, scrolling, handling alerts and frames
other.Supported languages (not exhaustive list: Java, Python, JavaScript, C#, Ruby and others

software_e2e_testing_tools.name: Playwright
software_e2e_testing_tools.description: Modern cross-browser end-to-end testing framework maintained by Microsoft, designed for reliable automation of web applications

other.Interactions: Mouse clicks and double-clicks, hover, drag and drop, typing, file uploads, multi-tab and multi-page scenarios, mobile emulation

software_e2e_testing_tools.name: Puppeteer
software_e2e_testing_tools.description: Node.js library for controlling Chromium and other browsers via DevTools or WebDriver BiDi, often used for lightweight end-to-end tests and browser automation

other.Interactions: Clicks, typing, scrolling, file uploads, interaction with dialogs, capturing screenshots and PDFs, collecting performance metrics

software_e2e_testing_tools.name: WebdriverIO
software_e2e_testing_tools.description: Open-source automation framework for Node.js that wraps WebDriver and WebDriver BiDi, supporting web, mobile and component testing

other.Interactions: Clicks, typing, element selection, scrolling, drag and drop, navigation, assertions, interaction with native browser dialogs

software_e2e_testing_tools.name: TestCafe
software_e2e_testing_tools.description: End-to-end testing framework for web applications that does not require WebDriver or browser plugins and runs on Node.js

other.Interactions: Clicks, typing, drag and drop, file upload, navigation, screenshots, execution in multiple local and remote browsers

software_e2e_testing_tools.name: Nightwatch.js
software_e2e_testing_tools.description: Integrated end-to-end testing framework for web applications based on the W3C WebDriver API, with built-in test runner

other.Interactions: Clicks, navigation, form filling, assertions on DOM elements, screenshot capture, interaction with iframes and windows

software_e2e_testing_tools.name: Appium
software_e2e_testing_tools.description: Open-source automation framework for testing native, hybrid and mobile web applications on iOS, Android and other platforms using the WebDriver protocol

other.Interactions: Touches and taps, long presses, swipes, scrolls, typing, multi-touch gestures, navigation, interaction with system dialogs and mobile web views
other.Supported languages (not exhaustive list: Java, JavaScript, Python, Ruby, C#, PHP and others via client libraries

software_static_analysis_tools.name: Semgrep
software_static_analysis_tools.description: Multi-language static analysis tool with customizable rules.

software_static_analysis_tools.name: SonarQube (Community Edition)
software_static_analysis_tools.description: Comprehensive code quality and security analysis platform. Community edition does not cover all languages/features.

other.GitHub integration: Partial (Community: main-branch analysis only; PR decoration requires paid editions)

software_static_analysis_tools.name: CodeQL
software_static_analysis_tools.description: Semantic code analysis engine developed by GitHub; focuses on security vulnerabilities.

other.License (for use in open source platform: MIT (queries/libraries); CodeQL CLI under GitHub CodeQL license/terms

software_static_analysis_tools.name: Lizard
software_static_analysis_tools.description: Cyclomatic Complexity Analyzer for many programming languages includingC#, C/C++, Java, JavaScript, Python and TypeScript.

software_static_analysis_tools.name: PMD
software_static_analysis_tools.description: Static analyzer for Java and other languages (includes JS support).

software_static_analysis_tools.name: cppcheck
software_static_analysis_tools.description: Static analysis tool for C and C++ detecting bugs and undefined behavior.

software_static_analysis_tools.name: Clang-Tidy
software_static_analysis_tools.description: Part of LLVM/Clang project; performs linting and static analysis for C/C++/Objective-C.

software_static_analysis_tools.name: Infer
software_static_analysis_tools.description: Static analysis tool from Meta for C, C++, Java, and Objective-C focused on bug detection.

software_static_analysis_tools.name: OCLint
software_static_analysis_tools.description: Analyzes C, C++, and Objective-C code for potential issues and style violations.

software_performance_tools.name: Google Lighthouse
software_performance_tools.dimensions_covered: performance (load times, Core Web Vitals), accessibility, best practices, SEO
software_performance_tools.description: Automated auditing tool for web pages that reports performance, accessibility, best practices and SEO scores based on lab tests in a real or headless browser

other.Resource type measured: browser loading behavior, DOM rendering, JavaScript execution
other.Delivery model: CLI tool, browser DevTools integration, Node.js library, CI integrations

software_performance_tools.name: WebPageTest (self-hosted)
software_performance_tools.dimensions_covered: performance (page load, rendering, Core Web Vitals), network behavior, caching efficiency
software_performance_tools.description: Deep web performance testing and diagnostics tool that runs scripted browser sessions and produces detailed waterfalls, visual metrics and Core Web Vitals

other.Resource type measured: HTTP requests, browser resource loading, DNS/SSL negotiation, rendering timeline
other.Open source: mixed (Polyform Shield on main branch; Apache 2.0 available via apache branch)
other.Delivery model: self-hosted web UI and API, optional public SaaS

software_performance_tools.name: PageSpeed Insights
software_performance_tools.dimensions_covered: performance (Core Web Vitals), UX quality for real users, best practices
software_performance_tools.description: Free Google service that combines Lighthouse lab tests with field data from the Chrome User Experience Report to rate page performance on mobile and desktop

other.Resource type measured: browser loading behavior, real-user timing data (RUM)

software_performance_tools.name: Sitespeed.io
software_performance_tools.dimensions_covered: performance (load times, Web Vitals, visual metrics), network behavior, JavaScript execution cost
software_performance_tools.description: Open-source toolkit that orchestrates browsers and tools such as Browsertime and Lighthouse to collect and store web performance metrics over time

other.Resource type measured: browser loading behavior, HTTP requests, rendering and visual changes
other.Delivery model: CLI tool with self-hosted web UI and integrations to time-series backends

software_performance_tools.name: Apache JMeter
software_performance_tools.description: Open-source Java application for load, stress and functional testing of web applications and a wide range of networked services

other.Target layer: backend / API, web frontend (HTTP), other protocols
other.Target environment: web application, service / API, microservices, databases and messaging systems
other.Resource type measured: HTTP endpoints, TCP/UDP services, JDBC, JMS, FTP, and other protocol traffic
other.Delivery model: desktop GUI, CLI tool, self-hosted test engine

software_security_tools.name: OWASP Application Security Verification Standard (ASVS)
software_security_tools.description: A standard that defines security requirements and verification levels for designing, building, and testing secure web applications and APIs.

other.Security dimensions: Application and API security controls; secure coding requirements

software_security_tools.name: OWASP Web Security Testing Guide (WSTG)
software_security_tools.description: A comprehensive guide that provides methodologies and test cases for performing web application security testing and penetration testing.

other.Security dimensions: Application and API security controls; penetration testing coverage

software_security_tools.name: OWASP Zed Attack Proxy (ZAP)
software_security_tools.description: An open-source dynamic application security testing tool for finding vulnerabilities in running web applications through automated and manual testing.

other.Security dimensions: Application and API runtime security; web vulnerability exposure

software_security_tools.name: Bandit (Python security linter)
software_security_tools.description: A static analysis tool that identifies common security issues and insecure coding practices in Python source code.

other.Security dimensions: Secure coding practices for Python-based components

software_security_tools.name: OWASP Software Assurance Maturity Model (SAMM)
software_security_tools.description: A framework for measuring and improving the maturity of secure software development practices across governance, design, implementation, and operations.

other.Security dimensions: Secure development lifecycle maturity; security governance and process quality

software_security_tools.name: CIS Critical Security Controls
software_security_tools.description: A prioritized set of best-practice security controls to improve an organisation’s overall cybersecurity posture and resilience.

other.Security dimensions: Cloud and infrastructure security; organisational security posture; incident response readiness

software_security_tools.name: CIS Benchmarks
software_security_tools.description: Configuration best practices and hardening guidelines for operating systems, cloud services, containers, and applications.

other.Security dimensions: Cloud and infrastructure configuration hardening; secure configuration management

software_security_tools.name: Cloud Security Alliance Cloud Controls Matrix (CCM) and CAIQ
software_security_tools.description: A cloud security control framework and questionnaire that helps assess and communicate cloud security capabilities and assurance.

other.Security dimensions: Cloud security controls; transparency and assurance for cloud consumers

software_security_tools.name: ENISA Cloud Computing Security Risk Assessment and Information Assurance Framework
software_security_tools.description: Guidance and frameworks from ENISA for assessing, managing, and assuring cloud computing security risks.

other.Security dimensions: Cloud and infrastructure security risk; alignment with EU cloud-assurance expectations
other.Type of tool: Risk assessment methodology + assurance criteria framework

software_security_tools.name: OWASP Dependency-Check
software_security_tools.description: A tool that identifies known vulnerabilities in third-party dependencies by scanning project dependency manifests.

other.Security dimensions: Supply chain and third-party dependency security; vulnerable component exposure

software_security_tools.name: OSV-Scanner
software_security_tools.description: A command-line tool that scans source code, dependencies, and SBOMs for known vulnerabilities using the OSV database.

other.Security dimensions: Supply chain security; vulnerability exposure in open-source dependencies and SBOMs

software_security_tools.name: Trivy
software_security_tools.description: A security scanner for containers, cloud-native workloads, dependencies, and infrastructure-as-code configurations.

other.Security dimensions: Container and cloud-native workload security; dependency vulnerabilities; infrastructure-as-code misconfigurations

software_security_tools.name: SLSA (Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts)
software_security_tools.description: A framework that defines maturity levels and best practices for securing software supply chains and build processes.

other.Security dimensions: Software supply-chain integrity; build and release process maturity

software_security_tools.name: CHAOSS Libyears and related risk metrics
software_security_tools.description: A set of metrics that quantify how outdated software dependencies are and help assess open-source dependency risk.

other.Security dimensions: Dependency freshness; open-source project risk and sustainability

software_security_tools.name: OpenSSF Scorecard
software_security_tools.description: An automated tool that assesses open-source projects against a set of security best practices and produces a security score.

other.Security dimensions: Open-source project security practices; governance and automation quality

software_security_tools.name: OWASP DefectDojo
software_security_tools.description: An open-source vulnerability management platform that aggregates, tracks, and prioritises security findings from multiple tools.

other.Security dimensions: Vulnerability management; remediation workflow; overall application security posture

software_security_tools.name: Bearer CLI
software_security_tools.description: Bearer CLI is a static application security testing (SAST) tool that scans your source code and analyzes your data flows to discover, filter and prioritize security and privacy risks.

ontology_tools.name: Protégé Desktop
ontology_tools.dimensions_covered: Usability and Understandability; Interoperability and Compatibility; Sustainability
ontology_tools.description: Free, open-source OWL ontology editor with built-in integration to DL reasoners and support for debugging inconsistencies and refactoring ontologies.

other.Main assessment use: Ontology editing; structural inspection; consistency checking through integrated reasoners; explanation and debugging of modeling issues

ontology_tools.name: WebProtégé
ontology_tools.dimensions_covered: Usability and Understandability; Sustainability; Usage and Impact
ontology_tools.description: Web-based collaborative ontology development environment supporting OWL 2, permissions, discussions, and change tracking for team-based ontology maintenance.

other.Main assessment use: Collaborative editing; discussion and review; change tracking; governance and versioning support

ontology_tools.name: OOPS! (OntOlogy Pitfall Scanner!)
ontology_tools.dimensions_covered: Usability and Understandability; Interoperability and Compatibility; Sustainability
ontology_tools.description: Web-based ontology evaluation tool that detects common ontology “pitfalls” that can lead to modeling errors and quality issues.

other.Main assessment use: Detection of common modeling pitfalls; ontology quality diagnostics; identification of best-practice violations

ontology_tools.name: HermiT OWL Reasoner
ontology_tools.description: OWL 2 DL reasoner that checks ontology consistency and computes class hierarchies (classification) and related inferences.

ontology_tools.name: ELK Reasoner
ontology_tools.description: High-performance OWL 2 EL reasoner for large ontologies; used to classify and validate profile-consistent ontologies efficiently.

other.Main assessment use: High-performance consistency checking and classification for ontologies within the OWL 2 EL profile

ontology_tools.name: Openllet (Pellet-derived) Reasoner
ontology_tools.description: OWL 2 reasoner (Pellet lineage) for checking ontology consistency, classification, and inference services.

ontology_tools.name: OntoMetrics
ontology_tools.description: Web-based tool that validates and displays statistics/metrics about a given ontology (upload OWL/RDF and obtain metric summaries).

other.Main assessment use: Ontology statistics; size and complexity profiling; metric-based summaries

ontology_tools.name: Ontospy
ontology_tools.description: Python library/CLI for inspecting and visualizing RDF/OWL vocabularies, extracting ontology structure and generating lightweight documentation.

other.Main assessment use: Schema inspection; vocabulary profiling; visualization of ontology structure; lightweight documentation support

ontology_tools.name: WIDOCO
ontology_tools.dimensions_covered: Usability and Understandability; Sustainability; Interoperability and Compatibility
ontology_tools.description: Wizard to generate enriched, customizable ontology documentation (terms, sections, visualizations) for publishing and reuse.

other.Main assessment use: Documentation generation; publishing readiness; support for understandability, reuse, and dissemination

ontology_tools.name: Pletka
ontology_tools.dimensions_covered: Usability and Understandability; Interoperability and Compatibility; Sustainability
ontology_tools.description: Pletka is a platform for collaboratively creating, documenting, and sharing semantic data models.

ontology_tools.name: Framework for Ontology Quality Assessment (FOCA)
ontology_tools.dimensions_covered: Comprehensiveness; Usability and Understandability; Interoperability and Compatibility; Sustainability
ontology_tools.description: Methodology for ontology evaluation based on a Goal–Question–Metric approach. It includes a questionnaire and a scoring model to assess ontology quality beyond formal consistency, with questions about completeness, reuse, documentation, organizational fitness, and alignment.

other.Main assessment use: Questionnaire-based and score-based ontology quality assessment beyond formal validation

ontology_tools.name: Ontology Usability Scale (OUS)
ontology_tools.dimensions_covered: Usability and Understandability; Usage and Impact
ontology_tools.description: A questionnaire-style usability instrument proposed specifically for ontologies, with short statements intended to assess ontology usability in terms of effectiveness, efficiency, satisfaction, contextual relevance, maturity, intended use, and fitness for use. It is especially suitable for expert/user evaluation workshops or pilot-based assessment.

other.Main assessment use: Expert- and user-based assessment of ontology usability, fitness for use, and practical adoption

ontology_tools.name: QASAR
ontology_tools.dimensions_covered: Comprehensiveness; Usability and Understandability; Interoperability and Compatibility; Sustainability
ontology_tools.description: Recent ontology quality-assurance platform that integrates multiple QA methodologies, interactive reports, and guided recommendations to support broader ontology evaluation.

other.Main assessment use: Integrated ontology quality-assurance workflow; guided review; interactive reporting; broader ontology evaluation support

kg_tools.name: Shapes Constraint Language (SHACL)
kg_tools.description: A W3C language for validating RDF graphs against a set of conditions. These conditions are provided as shapes and other constructs expressed in the form of an RDF graph.

kg_tools.name: Apache Jena SHACL
kg_tools.description: Apache implementation of the W3C SHACL. It implements SHACL Core and SHACL SPARQL Constraints.

kg_tools.name: pySHACL
kg_tools.description: Python-based SHACL validator for RDF graphs.

kg_tools.name: SHACL Playground
kg_tools.description: A constraint validator for the Shapes Constraint Language, written in JavaScript.

kg_tools.name: SHACL Play!
kg_tools.description: Free suite of tools from Sparna to work with SHACL.

kg_tools.name: Shape Expressions
kg_tools.description: A structural schema language for validation, traversal and transformation of RDF graphs.

kg_tools.name: RDFUnit
kg_tools.description: Rule-based validation framework using SPARQL test patterns to detect structural issues and missing links.

kg_tools.name: F-UJI
kg_tools.description: A web service to programatically assess FAIRness of research data objects based on metrics developed by the FAIRsFAIR project.

kg_tools.name: FAIR-Checker
kg_tools.description: A web and command line tool to assess FAIRness of web resources.

kg_tools.name: FAIR Evaluator
kg_tools.description: Resources and guidelines to assess the FAIRness of digital resources.

kg_tools.name: FAIR-Shake
kg_tools.description: Combines automated FAIR checks with human-evaluated rubrics.

kg_tools.name: TripleCheckMate
kg_tools.description: Crowdsourcing platform for human validation of RDF triples selected automatically.

kg_tools.name: CrowdHeritage
kg_tools.dimensions_covered: Context and Relevance; Richness; Multilingual Labeling Coverage; Semantic Accuracy; Interpretability
kg_tools.description: Open crowdsourcing platform for cultural heritage institutions to run campaigns in which contributors enrich and validate digitised cultural heritage metadata by adding annotations or reviewing existing ones.

kg_tools.name: Europeana methodology for validating enrichments
kg_tools.dimensions_covered: Factual; Semantic; Context and Relevance; Completeness
kg_tools.description: Methodology used by the Europeana Foundation to validate metadata enrichment efforts before acceptance. It combines documentation of the enrichment context, representative sampling, manual or semi-automated assessment of sample outputs, validation against agreed quality requirements, and, where relevant, calibration of confidence thresholds and iterative improvement of the enrichment process.
